claim: Chen et al. reported that duplex lightweight steel Fe–8Mn–6Al–0.4C had a tensile strength of ~756 MPa and ~56% elongation due to synergistic TRIP and TWIP effects.
evidence_text: Chen et al. [27] reported that duplex lightweight steel of Fe–8Mn–6Al–0.4C exhibited excellent mechanical properties of tensile strength ~756 MPa and ~56 % elongation under synergy of TRIP and TWIP effects.

claim: Under equilibrium cooling, δ-ferrite (BCC_A2) begins to precipitate from the liquid at approximately 1485 °C.
evidence_text: δ-ferrite (δ-F, BCC_A2 in Fig. 2(a)) began to precipitate in the steel liquid (L) at around 1485 ◦C with the decrease of temperature.

claim: Austenite (FCC_A1) forms at around 1390 °C via the peritectic reaction δ-ferrite + liquid → austenite.
evidence_text: As long as temperature dropping to around $1 3 9 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ austenite (A, FCC_A1 in Fig. 2(a)) formed by the peritectic reaction, $\delta \mathrm { - } \mathrm { F } + \mathrm { L } \to \mathrm { A } ,$ with the solidification of the steel liquid.

claim: The maximum equilibrium austenite fraction is ~91.4% and the minimum δ-ferrite fraction is ~8.6%, both occurring at around 970 °C.
evidence_text: Until the temperature going down to around ${ 9 7 0 ^ { \circ } } \mathrm { C } ,$ Austenite fraction reached to maximum, around 91.4 %, while δ-ferrite fraction reached to minimum, around 8.6 %.

claim: Room-temperature tensile tests were conducted at a strain rate of 0.375 min^{-1} on rod-shaped specimens of M10×60 mm with a gauge length of 25 mm, with the tensile axis parallel to the rolling direction.
evidence_text: tensile properties of the steel under various treatment states were measured using the electronic tensile testing machine (WDW-100E) at the strain rate o $\mathrm { { f } } 0 . 3 7 5 \mathrm { { m i n } } ^ { - 1 }$ , with rod-shaped sample size of M$[ 1 0 ^ { * } 6 0$ mm and gauge length of 25 mm. The tensile axis was parallel to the rolling direction of the hot-rolled plate.

claim: XRD for austenite content measurement used Cu-Kα radiation at 40 kV and 150 mA, with a step size of 0.02° and scan speed of 2°/min; scanning areas were 10×10 mm before and 5×20 mm after tensile tests.
evidence_text: X-ray diffraction (SmartLab) with Cu-Kα radiation at 40 kV and 150 mA was conducted to measure austenite content, with scanning step size of 0.02◦ and scanning speed of 2◦/min. XRD specimens with scanning areas of 10 * 10 mm before tensile tests and $5 ~ ^ { \ast } ~ 2 0$ mm after tensile tests were prepared...

claim: During holding at 1050 °C for IQ-1050, prior austenite grains grow and prior austenite grain boundaries move into δ-ferrite, making δ-ferrite bands thinner and dividing them into discontinuous grains.
evidence_text: prior austenite grain grew up as lath martensite reversed in the holding process at 1 0 5 0 °C, and prior austenite grain boundary (PAGB) moved to δ-ferrite. Accordingly, the δ-ferrite bands became thinner, and even were divided into some discontinuous δ-ferrite grains by prior austenite

claim: Carbides, considered to be cementite, precipitate and widely distribute in tempered martensite only when annealing temperature is 620 °C.
evidence_text: Carbides (Cm), which were considered to be cementite, were precipitated and widely distributed in tempered martensite, only when annealing temperature was 6 2 0 °C, as shown in Fig. 3(e).

claim: Annealing at 800 °C causes austenite grains to grow and dilutes C and Mn, thereby increasing Ms temperature and causing martensitic transformation during air cooling.
evidence_text: As the annealing temperature increasing to 800 °C, small austenite grains grew up, and C, Mn elements in the austenite were diluted. Therefore, Ms temperature of austenite increased, resulting in Martensite transformation during air cooling.

claim: In band contrast maps, brighter regions correspond to ferrite (high BC value) and darker regions to lath martensite (low BC value); tempered martensite in QA specimens shows higher BC than martensite due to recovery and dislocation reduction.
evidence_text: The brighter and whiter regions due to high BC value were consider as ferrite, while darker regions due to low BC value were consider as lath martensite. Tempered martensite in QA series possessed relative higher BC value than martensite, which was related to recovery of martensite in annealing treatment leading to decreasing of dislocations.

claim: After heating at 1050 °C, δ-ferrite and prior austenite grains grow, and ferrite bands become segmented by martensite lamellae, a morphology retained after annealing.
evidence_text: According to Fig. 4(a, b), δ-ferrite and prior austenite grains grew up after heating of 1050 °C, and ferrite bands were segmented by martensite lamellar, becoming dispersed. This situation was kept in annealed microstructure.

claim: Austenite volume fraction increases with annealing temperature from 620 to 740 °C; QA-800 fraction is slightly lower than QA-740, probably due to martensitic transformation during air cooling.
evidence_text: With increase of annealing temperature at the range of 620-740 °C, austenitic proportion increased. However, the difference of austenitic volume fraction between QA-740 and QA-800 was unobvious, and even the fraction of QA-800 was lower, which could be associated with martensite transformation of the latter when air cooling.

claim: The hot-rolled steel shows the highest tensile strength of ~1107 MPa and poor total elongation of ~5.6%.
evidence_text: The hot-rolled steel showed the highest tensile strength (TS) of ~1107 MPa and poor total elongation (TE) of ~5.6 %.

claim: After intercritical quenching, yield strength is improved to ~575 MPa, tensile strength declines to ~929 MPa, and total elongation is the poorest at ~2.0%.
evidence_text: After intercritical quenching, the yield strength (YS) of the steel was improved, ~575 MPa, compared with HR, but TS was declined, ~929 MPa, and TE of ~2.0 % was poorest among all of specimens.

claim: Annealing considerably enhances total elongation: QA-620 ~44%, QA-680 ~45.6%, QA-740 ~62.4%, QA-800 ~50.4%.
evidence_text: With the annealing temperature increasing, TEs of QA series steels were ~44 %, ~45.6 %, ~62.4 %, and ~50.4 %, respectively.

claim: Uniform elongation of all QA specimens except QA-680 is almost equal to total elongation; QA-680 exhibits pronounced necking when strain reaches around 25%.
evidence_text: Uniform elongation of all QA specimens, except QA-680, was almost equal to total elongation. For QA-680, When strain reached to around 25 %, the stress began to decrease, which meant that there was significantly necking in the tensile specimen.

claim: The product of tensile strength and total elongation (PSE) of all QA specimens exceeds 30 GPa%, and QA-740 achieves the highest PSE of ~45.2 GPa%.
evidence_text: The products of tensile strength and total elongation (PSE) of all QA specimens were more than 30 GPa%, showing superior comprehensive mechanical properties. PSE of QA-740, ~45.2 GPa%, was the highest one

claim: At initial strain ε < 0.01, the work hardening rate sharply decreases due to deformation of soft ferrite and dislocation movement.
evidence_text: At the initial stage of ε < 0.01, the WHR sharply decreases, which was mainly related to deformation of soft phase, for example, ferrite, and dislocation movement.

claim: For ε > 0.01, the work hardening rate curves decrease gradually, attributed to a hardening mechanism.
evidence_text: For ε > 0.01, WHR curves got used to slightly decrease, which was attributed to hardening mechanism.

claim: Work hardening rate curves of all QA specimens are almost superimposed for ε < 0.15.
evidence_text: WHR curves of all QA specimens were almost superimposed at the range of ε < 0.15.

claim: The austenite volume fractions measured by EBSD in the experimental steel were approximately 16% for HR, 7% for IQ-1050, 28% for QA-620, 35% for QA-680, 50% for QA-740, and 49% for QA-800.
The austenite volume fractions measured by XRD were approximately 33% for QA-620, 37% for QA-680, 51% for QA-740, and 48% for QA-800.

Hot-rolled (HR) specimen had yield strength 305 ± 11 MPa, tensile strength 1107 ± 32 MPa, total elongation 5.6 ± 0.4%, yield-to-tensile ratio 0.28, and product of strength and elongation 6.2 ± 0.6 GPa%.
IQ-1050 specimen had yield strength 575 ± 13 MPa, tensile strength 929 ± 30 MPa, total elongation 2.0 ± 0.3%, yield-to-tensile ratio 0.62, and PSE 1.9 ± 0.4 GPa%.

QA-740 achieved the best ductility among all specimens with yield strength 498 ± 3 MPa, tensile strength 725 ± 9 MPa, total elongation 62.4 ± 4.2%, yield-to-tensile ratio 0.69, and PSE 45.2 ± 3.7 GPa%.

At true strain 0.0, all QA specimens exhibited an initial work hardening rate of approximately 5000 MPa; at true strain 0.1, the WHR dropped to 1500 MPa for QA-620, 1400 MPa for QA-680, 1300 MPa for QA-740, and 1400 MPa for QA-800.
evidence_text: True strain/ε QA-620 (Work hardening rate/Mpa) QA-680 (Work hardening rate/Mpa) QA-740 (Work hardening rate/Mpa) QA-800 (Work hardening rate/Mpa) 0.0 5000 5000 5000 5000 0.1 1500 1400 1300 1400

claim: The hot-rolled specimen HR had the highest GND density (BCC: 10.81×10^14 m^-2, FCC: 8.37×10^14 m^-2), which contributed to high tensile strength (1107 ± 32 MPa) but poor ductility (5.6%).
evidence_text: The microstructure of HR subjected to hot-rolling conditions showed the highest GND density of BCC phases and FCC phase. ... which gave the steel high tensile strength (1107 ± 32 MPa) but poor ductility (5.6% ± 0.4).

claim: In IQ-1050, the GND densities were 8.23×10^14 m^-2 for BCC and 5.01×10^14 m^-2 for FCC; more lath martensite induced stress concentration and premature failure, resulting in lower tensile strength (929 ± 30 MPa) and poor ductility (2.0%).
evidence_text: During quenching processing of 1050 °C, all martensite in the investigated hot-rolled steel reversed into austenite... More lath martensite in IQ-1050 than HR contributed to higher yield strength (575 ± 13 MPa), but more easily induced stress concentration during deformation causing premature failure. Therefore, the IQ-1050 had a poorer tensile strength (929 ± 30 MPa) and ductility (2.0 % ± 0.3).

claim: With increasing annealing temperature from 620 to 800 °C, the GND density of BCC phase decreased from 5.63 to 4.63 ×10^14 m^-2 and FCC phase from 3.39 to 2.66 ×10^14 m^-2, which lowered the yield strength by facilitating dislocation slip.
evidence_text: With the increase of annealing temperature, the martensitic and austenitic GND values of annealed specimens decreased continuously. ... decreasing the yield strength of tempered martensite lamellar.

claim: The decreasing density of block boundaries (>45°) with increasing annealing temperature contributed to the decrease in yield strength of annealed specimens.
evidence_text: the decrease of block boundary with angle of >45° in density, to some extent, contributed to the decrease of yield strength of the annealed specimens, with the increase of annealed temperature

claim: The GND density of austenite in HR and IQ-1050 was comparable to that reported for strained Hadfield austenitic steel under true strain of ~0.05 (~9.80×10^14 m^-2).
evidence_text: austenite of HR and IQ-1050 had similar GND density values compared to that reported for strained Hadfield austenitic steel [43,44] under true strain of ~0.05, whose GND density value was around 9.80 × 10^14 / m^2.

claim: Austenitic grain (AG) boundary density increased with annealing temperature up to 740 °C (1.6 µm^-1) but decreased at 800 °C (1.25 µm^-1) due to coarse austenite grains.
evidence_text: For QA specimens, with the increase of annealed temperature, AG boundary density increased. When annealed temperature being 800 °C, the density instead decreased due to some coarse austenite grains.

claim: A Python machine learning model based on crystallographic information was used to correctly identify the various boundary types and exactly calculate the boundary density for the specimens, as shown in Fig. 7.
evidence_text: A Python machine learning model developed by Li et al. [48] based on crystallographic information were used to correctly identify the various boundary types and exactly calculate the boundary density, as shown in Fig. 7(a-2,b-2, c-2,d-2,e-2,f-2).

claim: After quenching at 1050 °C, the density of block boundary was higher than that of AG boundary in IQ-1050, opposite to HR, indicating that more austenitic fraction underwent martensitic transformation in IQ-1050 than in HR.
evidence_text: After quenching of 1050 °C, the density of block boundary was higher than that of AG boundary, and the situation of HR was opposite, which indicated that more austenitic fraction underwent martensitic transformation than that of HR.

claim: In QA specimens, AG boundary density increased with annealing temperature up to 740 °C, but decreased at 800 °C due to some coarse austenite grains; meanwhile, block boundary density decreased continuously with annealing temperature.
evidence_text: For QA specimens, with the increase of annealed temperature, AG boundary density increased. When annealed temperature being 800 °C, the density instead decreased due to some coarse austenite grains. Meanwhile, block boundary density decreased continuously with annealed temperature.

claim: The variation trend of AG boundary density was consistent with that of austenitic volume fraction, but it did not play an effective role in enhancing strength; the decrease of block boundary density (>45°) contributed to the decrease of yield strength of the annealed specimens with increasing annealed temperature.
evidence_text: Obviously, the variation trend of AG boundary density was consistent with that of austenitic volume fraction, but it did not play an effectively role in enhancing strength. ... It was considered that the decrease of block boundary with angle of >45° in density, to some extent, contributed to the decrease of yield strength of the annealed specimens, with the increase of annealed temperature.

claim: The SFEs of QA specimens were between 20 mJ/m² and 40 mJ/m², which meant that TWIP effect was supposed to exist in austenite during deformation.
evidence_text: The SFEs of QA specimens were between 20 mJ/m² and 40 mJ/m², which meant that TWIP effect was supposed to exist in austenite when deformation.

claim: TRIP effect also occurred during deformation of QA specimens, although SFEs >20 mJ/m², because austenitic fraction after tensile test was lower than that before tensile test.
evidence_text: It was concluded that TRIP effect, in addition to TWIP effect, also occurred during deformation, although the SFEs >20 mJ/m², because austenitic fraction after tensile test was lower than that before tensile test.

claim: The austenitic mechanical stability constant k values, derived from V_ε = V_0 exp(-kε), were ~1.24 for QA-620, ~0.56 for QA-680, ~1.47 for QA-740, and ~1.50 for QA-800, where higher k indicates lower stability (Fig. 9(c)).

The stability of austenite was related to the enrichment of alloy elements, temperature, and grain size: in QA-620, carbide precipitation reduced alloy elements in austenite, lowering stability compared to QA-680; above 680 °C, increasing annealing temperature diluted alloying elements due to higher austenite fraction, decreasing stability; smaller austenite grains had higher enrichment and stability.
evidence_text: The stability of austenite was related to the enrichment of alloy elements, temperature, and grain size [52,55,56]. For QA-620, the precipitation of carbides led to a decrease of alloy elements in austenite, which diminished its austenitic stability, compared to QA-680. ... Above 680 °C, austenite fraction increased with the increasing of annealing temperature, which resulted in that the alloying elements in austenite were diluted. Thus, austenitic stability decreased with annealing temperature. ... Smaller grains had higher enrichment of alloy elements, and their stability was higher, while larger austenite grains possessed lower stability.

claim: When some austenite transformed into martensite during deformation, some of the remaining austenite exhibited TWIP effect.
evidence_text: when some austenite transformed into martensite during deformation, some of the rest of austenite should produce TWIP effect.

claim: In stage I, WHR sharply decreased due to increased dislocation mean free path in δ-ferrite resulting from grain growth and decreased grain boundary density after heating at 1050 °C and annealing.
evidence_text: after heating process of 1050 °C and annealed treatment, δ-ferrite grain apparently grew up, while small grains were gradually engulfed by larger grains. Hence, the grain boundary density of ferrite apparently decreased, while dislocation mean free path increased. Derived from large dislocation mean free path of ferrite, the WHR sharply decreased.

claim: In stage II, the decline of WHR slowed down due to competing hardening mechanisms (grain boundary and dislocation tangle hindrance, TRIP effect) and softening mechanisms (dislocation gliding, annihilation at grain boundaries, cross slip).
evidence_text: In stage II, the decline of the WHR obviously slowed down, which attributed to several hardening mechanisms. The hindrance of grain boundaries and high-density dislocation tangles to dislocation movement, as well as TRIP effect, could enhance WHR. Meanwhile, dislocation gliding played a mainly role in reducing WHR. Moreover, the finite dislocation annihilation at grain boundaries, as well as cross slip among dislocations, were also expected to contribute to lowering WHR.

claim: In stage III, WHR increased slowly as TRIP effect dominated, with a larger number of small austenite grains transforming to martensite, generating a stronger hardening effect akin to second-phase particle strengthening.
evidence_text: As true strain increasing during stage III, a larger number of small austenite grains transformed into martensite, producing stronger hardening effect to make WHR enduringly increasing. The martensite produced during the strain process not only acted as an obstacle of dislocation gliding, but also as a site of dislocation accumulation and a source of partial dislocations. Further, the martensite due to fine austenite grains, as small particles of hard phase, distributed widely in tempered martensite area, which could hinder dislocation gliding more strongly, boosting WHR. This was similar to second-phase particles strengthening mechanism.

claim: Finer austenite grains possessed higher enrichment of alloying elements and thus higher stability, requiring a certain critical strain for martensitic transformation.
evidence_text: The finer austenite grain possessed higher enrichment degree of alloy elements, and thus corresponding austenitic stability was higher. The fine austenite grains possessing different stability transformed into martensite only when a certain critical strain was attained.

claim: During tensile deformation, stress concentration at δ-ferrite/tempered martensite interfaces led to crack initiation; cracks were captured by δ-ferrite and propagated along δ-ferrite bands, forming deep valley-like secondary cracks (Fig. 6).
evidence_text: During tensile tests, ductility deformation led to dislocation accumulation at the interfaces of δ-ferrite band and tempered martensite lamellar. Due to the difference in hardness between the two phases, stress concentration was prone to occur at their interfaces, to induce crack initiation. Once the crack forms, it would be captured by δ-ferrite, and propagated into and along δ-ferrite band, leading to the formation of second cracks like deep valleys (Fig. 6).

claim: Crack propagation into δ-ferrite was blunted due to the soft δ-ferrite phase, providing high cracking resistance, and strain-induced martensitic transformation promoted crack closure by volume expansion.
evidence_text: the crack propagating into δ-ferrite could be blunted due to the soft phase of δ-ferrite, which produced high resistance to the cracking. Strain-induced martensitic transformation led to volume expansion, promoting the closure of cracks, which was positive to the ductility.

claim: Cleavage morphology formed in coarse δ-ferrite because deficient high-angle grain boundaries could not hinder microcrack propagation, consistent with {100} cleavage being a natural failure mode of BCC iron (Fig. 6).
evidence_text: Deficient high-angle grain boundaries of coarse δ-ferrite cannot effectively hinder microcrack propagation. Hence, cleavage morphology formed in δ-ferrite as microcrack propagating (Fig. 6). This was because {100} cleavage was a natural failure mode of many BCC crystals, including iron.

claim: After annealing at 800 °C, some austenite underwent martensitic transformation during air cooling, leading to a decrease in retained austenite fraction.
evidence_text: after annealing at 800 °C, some austenite underwent martensitic transformation during air cooling, which instead led to a decrease of austenite.

claim: At an annealing temperature of 620 °C, carbides precipitated in the tempered martensite region.
evidence_text: At 620 °C of annealing temperature, carbides precipitated in tempered martensite region.

claim: The 740 °C annealed steel achieved the highest product of strength and elongation of ~45.2 GPa% and the maximum uniform elongation of ~62.4%.
evidence_text: the 740 °C annealed steel had the highest product of strength and elongation, up to ~45.2 GPa%, and the maximum uniform elongation of ~62.4 %.

claim: Austenite in the specimen annealed at 680 °C possessed the highest mechanical stability, and stability decreased with increasing annealing temperature from 680 °C due to dilution of alloying elements, while at 620 °C carbide precipitation reduced matrix alloy content and lowered stability.
evidence_text: austenite in the specimen annealed at 680 °C possessed the highest stability. With annealing temperature increasing from 680 °C, austenitic stability decreased due to the dilution of austenitic alloy elements. When annealed at 620 °C, precipitated carbides in specimens led to the decrease of alloy elements in matrix, resulting in lower austenitic stability than that of 680 °C annealed specimens.
